title: Xia: Legends of a Drift System - Part Three - See You Space Cowboy...
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qiI-rwVGiYQ

Daniel (@dungeondive) closes his Xia: Legends of a Drift System trilogy with the end of his game and a verdict summed up simply: he absolutely loves it — like Explore! and Runebound fused into a science-fiction setting. He frames the affection through his beloved '80s/'90s space-opera anime (Crusher Joe, Dirty Pair) and a stack of "blue-collar space opera" reading recommendations (Gateway, Becky Chambers' The Long Way to a Small Angry Planet, the Planetes manga).

On gameplay, he praises the elegant NPC fame system: the opponents you race climb the track on a simple d20 roll each round — random, yet it works beautifully as a non-clock "timer" with rubber-banding catch-up events, keeping games close. He recounts his run as the Ghost Stalker upgraded to the Kemplar: turning outlaw to cross the planet Loathe and sell spice, dodging the Enforcer, and a Merchant ship whose market-manipulator behaviour kept clearing the economy board. He notes some frustration finishing missions when the needed sectors never appeared — a hazard of a short, exploration-driven game — but treasures the table's growth and the thrill of placing the blazing Xia star tile.

He discloses Xia was the first game voted in by Dungeon Dive Patreon members, and thanks them.

----

Does Xia's "NPCs climb the fame track on a die roll" rival system appeal as an elegant solo timer — or do you prefer a more deterministic race?
